children.text: United States agricultural exports have declined by more than 36 percent since 1981, from $43,800,000,000 in 1981 to $27,900,000,000 in 1987;
the United States share of the world market for agricultural commodities and products has dropped by 20 percent during the last 6 years;
for the first time in 15 years, the United States incurred monthly agricultural trade deficits in 1986;
the loss of $1,000,000,000 in United States agricultural exports causes the loss of 35,000 agricultural jobs and the loss of 60,000 nonagricultural jobs;
the loss of agricultural exports threatens family farms and the economic well-being of rural communities in the United States;

children.label: A
children.text: the addition of new exporting nations;

children.label: C
children.text: increased use of export subsidies designed to lower the price of commodities on the world market;

children.label: D
children.text: the existence of barriers to agricultural trade;

children.label: E
children.text: the slowdown in the growth of world food demand in the 1980’s due to cyclical economic factors, including currency fluctuations and a debt-related slowdown in the economic growth of agricultural markets in certain developing countries; and

children.label: F
children.text: the rapid buildup of surplus stocks as a consequence of favorable weather for agricultural production during the 1980’s;
increasing the volume and value of exports is important to the financial well-being of the farm sector in the United States and to increasing farm income in the United States;

in order to increase agricultural exports and improve prices for farmers and ranchers in the United States, it is necessary that all agricultural export programs of the United States be used in an expeditious manner, including programs established under the Food for Peace Act ( et seq.), the Commodity Credit Corporation Charter Act ( et seq.), and section 416 of the Agricultural Act of 1949 ();7 U.S.C. 169115 U.S.C. 7147 U.S.C. 1431

children.label: A
children.text: improve the handling, marketing, processing, storage, and distribution of imported agricultural commodities and products; and

children.label: B
children.text: increase livestock production to enhance the demand for United States feed grains;

food aid and export assistance programs in developing countries stimulate economic activity which causes incomes to rise, and, as incomes rise, diets improve and the demand for and ability to purchase food increases;
private voluntary organizations and cooperatives are important and successful partners in our food aid and development programs; and

children.label: A
children.text: provides communities with health care, credit systems, and tools for development; and

children.label: B
children.text: establishes the infrastructure that is essential to the expansion of markets for United States agricultural commodities and products.
